content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 5, 1991


Lowell Finley
REMCHO, JOHANSEN & PURCELL
220 Montgomery Street, Suite 800
San Francisco, CA  94104

                                    Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                         Our File No. A-91-180

Dear Mr. Finley:

     You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you on behalf of Speaker Willie L. Brown concerning the new
gift and honoraria limits of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹  Your letter seeks follow up advice to the Montgomery
Advice Letter, No. A-91-110 (copy enclosed). Please note that
this letter is based on the facts presented to us. In rendering
advice, the Commission does not act as the finder of fact. (In re
Ogelsby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, copy enclosed.)

     This letter confirms that your letter dated March 20, 1991,
accurately summarizes the telephone advice I provided to you. As
we discussed in our telephone conversation, the Act now provides
gift limits applicable to members of the Legislature and other
elected state officers. Section 89504(a) provides:

          (a) No elected state officer shall accept
     gifts from any single source in any calendar year
     with a total value of more than two hundred fifty
     dollars ($250).




_____________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. The Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-180
Page 2


A "gift" is defined as any payment to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received. (Section
82028(a).)

In our telephone conversation you stated that Speaker Brown's
campaign committee had assumed responsibility for the Washington
D.C. events discussed in our Montgomery Advice Letter (supra).
This was necessary because Assemblymember Roos left office. You
also stated that Speaker Brown is holding the events in order to
raise campaign funds. Funds left over after the events will be
treated as Speaker Brown's campaign funds.

You also stated that consistent with this characterization,
persons who contributed to the Speaker for the events were making
contributions to Speaker Brown to the full extent of the payments.
These contributions will be fully disclosed by Speaker Brown.
Persons who contribute more than a fixed amount will be provided
admission to the events, much like the purchase of a fundraising
ticket. Other members of the legislature and elected state
officers will be permitted to attend the events free of charge.

As I advised you in our telephone conversation, generally
free admission to an event provided to a public official is a
gift. (Robbins Advice Letter, No. 78-08-123, copy enclosed.)
However, Regulation 18726.8(b) (copy enclosed) provides:

A gift of a ticket or tickets or other
admission privileges to a specific fundraising
event shall be valued as set forth below.

* * *

(b) Political Fundraiser. Where the
event is a fundraising event for a campaign
committee or candidate, the ticket or other
admission privileges has no value.

Thus, based on your facts, I advised that free admission
provided to members of the Legislature or state elected officers
would not be reportable as a gift or contribution to the
recipient, nor would they be subject to the new gift limits of the
Act.

Travel and Accommodations

You also asked about the propriety of the Speaker using
campaign funds to pay for his own travel and accommodations for

Our File No. A-91-180
Page 3


the events.² Section 89513(a)(1) provides:

     (a) Campaign funds shall not be used to pay
or reimburse the candidate, the elected officer, or
employees or staff of the campaign committee or the
elected officer's governmental agency for travel
expenses and necessary accommodations except when
these expenditures are directly related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose.

     (1) For purposes of this section, pay-
ments or reimbursements for travel and
necessary accommodations shall be considered
as directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose if the
payments would meet standards similar to the
standards of the Internal Revenue Service
pursuant to Sections 162 and 274 of the
Internal Revenue Code for deductions of travel
expenses under the federal income tax law.

                (Emphasis added.)

     According to Section 89513(a)(1), the use of campaign funds
for payments or reimbursements for travel and necessary
accommodations is directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose if the payments meet standards similar to the
standards for deduction of travel expenses pursuant to
Internal Revenue Code Sections 162 and 274.

     Internal Revenue Code Section 162(a)(2) sets forth a general
rule which permits the deduction of all ordinary and necessary
travel expenses while away from home in the pursuit of a trade or
business. Therefore, if the travel expenses described in your
letter are ordinary and necessary expenses related to a political
purpose, use of campaign funds for the travel expenses and
accommodations is permitted.

     Please note, however that allowable accommodations are
limited to the duration of the conference. If accommodations are
provided for an additional period of time they will be considered
personal expenses that should be paid with personal funds unless
otherwise directly related to a political, legislative or
governmental purpose. Moreover, the Commission will begin
considering regulations interpreting the personal use laws and


² You stated in our telephone conversation and your letter that
the Speaker will not pay for any other official's travel and
accommodations but will only pay expenses associated with the
events in Washington D.C., including meeting room rentals,
flowers, entertainment and food.

Our File No. A-91-180
Page 4


gift limits of the Act in the near future. Therefore, it is
possible that the Commission may adopt regulations in the future
which may be at variance with the advice given in this letter.

I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter please feel free
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                   By: John W. Wallace
                                        Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:plh

Enclosures

sections.question_synthetic: The requestor seeks confirmation of telephone advice regarding gift limits under the Political Reform Act, the use of campaign funds for Speaker Brown's travel and accommodations for Washington D.C. fundraising events, and whether free admission to these events constitutes a reportable gift for legislators.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Free admission to fundraising events provided to legislators and state elected officers is not reportable as a gift or contribution under Regulation 18726.8(b). Campaign funds may be used for travel and accommodations if they meet IRS standards for ordinary and necessary business expenses related to a political purpose, though accommodations are limited to the event duration.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks traditional QUESTION and CONCLUSION section headers but contains verbatim statutory language and regulatory citations. Content clearly shows this is FPPC's response confirming prior telephone advice. Some OCR errors present (e.g., 'RElrtcHo' for likely 'Bainbridge') but do not significantly impact meaning.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The requestor seeks confirmation of telephone advice regarding gift limits under the Political Reform Act, the use of campaign funds for Speaker Brown's travel and accommodations for Washington D.C. fundraising events, and whether free admission to these events constitutes a reportable gift for legislators.

CONCLUSION: Free admission to fundraising events provided to legislators and state elected officers is not reportable as a gift or contribution under Regulation 18726.8(b). Campaign funds may be used for travel and accommodations if they meet IRS standards for ordinary and necessary business expenses related to a political purpose, though accommodations are limited to the event duration.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter confirming telephone advice to Speaker Willie L. Brown's counsel regarding the $250 gift limit for state elected officers and clarifying that campaign funds may be used for travel and accommodations to fundraising events if they meet IRS business expense standards, while free admission to fundraisers is not a reportable gift.
